label: Dry cracked forearms without infection or systemic skin-disease red flags
summary: Your symptoms fit dry, cracked skin on the forearms.
found: Your clinician diagnosed dry, cracked forearms skin. We did not find skin infection, open deep wound, severe rash, systemic illness, or another skin emergency today.
home: Use gentle skin care and avoid harsh soaps, scrubbing, or triggers that worsen the dryness.
Use moisturizer, protective covering, or activity limits only as your clinician recommended.
Avoid picking or scratching the cracked skin.
meds: Use creams, ointments, itch medicine, or other skin medicine only if prescribed or recommended.
Do not use leftover steroid, antibiotic, or antifungal medicine unless your clinician told you to.
return: Fever, spreading redness, warmth, pus, red streaks, rapidly worsening pain, or worsening swelling.
Skin peeling, mouth sores, a bodywide rash, or feeling very ill.
Symptoms that worsen or do not improve as expected.
follow: Follow up with primary care, urgent care, dermatology, or the ED if symptoms are not improving or your clinician told you to recheck.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of dry, cracked forearms skin or forearms xerosis.
No skin infection, deep open wound, severe cutaneous reaction, systemic illness, immunocompromised host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion: Skin infection, cellulitis, abscess, deep open wound, severe cutaneous adverse reaction, skin peeling or sloughing, mucosal lesions, systemic illness, immunocompromised host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed dermatology/wound plan.
must_not_miss: Systemic skin disease flare requiring separate care.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus supports dry-skin self-care with gentle skin care, moisturizers, avoiding irritants, and escalation for infection, severe rash, or systemic symptoms. This expansion is limited to clinician-diagnosed localized dry cracked skin with infection, deep wound, severe-rash, systemic, pregnancy/pediatric, immunocompromised, and specialist-directed pathways absent.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis of dry cracked forearms or forearms xerosis
localized dry cracked skin
no infection or deep open wound
no systemic or mucosal red flags

label: Localized sock-elastic contact rash without infection or anaphylaxis
summary: Your symptoms fit a localized skin reaction from sock elastic.
found: Your clinician diagnosed a localized sock elastic contact rash skin reaction. We did not find anaphylaxis, skin infection, mucosal involvement, or severe medication reaction today.
home: Avoid the suspected trigger if it is known.
Wash gently with mild soap and water, and avoid scrubbing the rash.
Use cool compresses, moisturizers, or coverings only as your clinician recommended.
meds: Use creams, ointments, itch medicine, or allergy medicine only if prescribed or recommended.
Do not use leftover steroid, antibiotic, or allergy medicine unless your clinician told you to.
return: Trouble breathing, throat tightness, swelling of the lips or tongue, fainting, or a bodywide reaction.
Fever, spreading redness, warmth, pus, red streaks, rapidly worsening rash, skin peeling, or mouth sores.
Symptoms that worsen or do not improve as expected.
follow: Follow up with primary care, urgent care, dermatology, allergy, or the ED if symptoms are not improving or your clinician told you to recheck.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of localized sock elastic contact rash skin reaction.
No anaphylaxis, systemic allergic reaction, mucosal involvement, skin infection, severe medication reaction, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion: Anaphylaxis, airway symptoms, hypotension, systemic allergic reaction, mucosal lesions, severe cutaneous adverse reaction, skin infection, rapidly progressive rash, immunocompromised host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed dermatology/allergy plan.

terms: clinician diagnosis localized sock elastic contact rash
discharge diagnosis localized sock elastic contact rash
localized sock elastic contact rash diagnosed

high_confidence_terms: clinician diagnosis localized sock elastic contact rash
localized sock elastic contact rash diagnosed
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus supports contact dermatitis framing from irritant or allergic exposures, trigger avoidance, gentle skin care, clinician-directed topical or allergy treatment, and escalation for severe allergic, infectious, or mucosal red flags. This expansion is limited to a clinician-diagnosed localized contact skin reaction with anaphylaxis, infection, mucosal, pregnancy/pediatric, severe-rash, and specialist-directed pathways absent.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis of localized sock elastic contact rash skin reaction
localized reaction only
no anaphylaxis
no infection, mucosal, or severe-rash red flags
